Equities rotated back into AI, memory, and storage names as renewed geopolitical tensions pushed oil prices higher, fueling stagflation concerns and pressuring sectors sensitive to rising energy costs. Regional banks, airlines, and materials underperformed. Semiconductors were supported by news surrounding $AVGO, while the DRAM index falling into the 55–60 range also triggered meaningful short covering. Meta's announcement of a new Canadian data center reinforced the view that AI infrastructure capacity remains insufficient, helping drive short covering in names such as $NBIS.

Treasuries sold off alongside higher oil prices, although crude at current levels is unlikely to materially affect inflation. The weakness in longer-duration bonds appears to be driven by other factors. Amazon's recent bond issuance was not particularly well received, raising concerns that continued AI-related debt issuance from hyperscalers could overwhelm market demand. At the same time, higher yields are likely to attract yield buyers, making it difficult to maintain outright short positions. A yield curve steepener still appears to be the preferred trade, though investors should note that if strong employment and persistent inflation continue, an earlier rate hike remains possible, as suggested in the FOMC minutes. The minutes explicitly cited AI-related capital investment as an inflationary force. If elevated oil prices and AI-driven GDP growth persist, rate hikes could come sooner than expected. However, that would likely bring forward the timing of tightening rather than raise the ultimate terminal rate.

THE VOL PICTURE

Wednesday's VIX rise was real, and that makes it different from everything else this week. VIX rose 0.77 to 16.90. Sticky Strike explained only 0.30 of it. The rest was genuine buying: Parallel Shift positive 0.25, the whole curve got more expensive. Put Skew positive 0.18, downside protection paid up. And the tell, Downside Convexity positive 0.25, the deep crash strikes got bought hard. That is Iran tail hedging. Upside strikes were sold to pay for it. Monday and Tuesday were mechanical noise. Wednesday was different. Traders paid up for real protection, and vol that rises for that reason does not come back down quickly.

At the same time the trailing tape went the other way. The biggest day in the trailing window rolled off Wednesday on schedule and 21 day realized dropped from 16.9 to 14.2 percent in one session. With VIX rising into that, the premium flipped from minus 0.8 to plus 2.7 in a single day. Options are now priced over the actual tape for the first time in weeks. Sellers of premium finally have cushion. On an ordinary tape realized falls to 12.2 percent by July 15 and 9.9 percent by July 22, and the premium builds toward plus 7 if VIX just holds here.

One warning on all of this. The falling vol math breaks if SPX has a single day that moves 1.5% or more in either direction, about 112 points. With the Iran situation, a day like that can arrive any morning. That leaves the market in a strange spot. Selling options finally pays well for the first time in weeks. But this is also the worst week to be short options if a war headline hits. Thursday's straddle at ±44 is the market pricing that exact tug of war.

WHAT HAPPENED WEDNESDAY

SPX closed 7482.71, down 21.14 points, and the close hides the day. The Dow dropped 576 points. The Nasdaq rose 0.2 percent as Nvidia and the chips rallied. SPX sat in the middle and finished with the third close at the 7483 level in five sessions. The market keeps coming home to that number.

Oil did the damage. Crude spiked as the US launched fresh strikes on Iran during market hours and Trump declared the ceasefire over. The fear is direct: rising energy costs feed inflation, and the FOMC Minutes released at 2:00 PM showed a Fed split on the direction of rates, with officials reluctant to cut until inflation progress is clearer. Higher oil into a divided Fed is the loop that pressured everything rate sensitive.

After the close the escalation continued. Trump threatened to bomb Iran and reimpose a naval blockade, said he is not sure he wants a deal at all, and oil rose another leg with WTI near 74.50, up about 4 percent in two days. His European allies are distancing themselves on Iran after a tense NATO summit. Despite all of it, futures sat near flat for the second straight night. The market's refusal to panic is itself a signal. Momentum stocks slipped into correction territory. Levi fell 5.5 percent after hours despite beating and raising guidance, a tell about stretched sentiment. SpaceX closed at 148, below its debut price, in a two day slide since joining the Nasdaq 100. Asia opened split with the Nikkei up nearly 2 percent.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR THURSDAY

The map changed a lot from Wednesday to Thursday, and the changes matter. On Wednesday, traders were buying protection at 7475 and 7425, and both levels broke fast when price fell into them. For Thursday those same levels flipped. Traders are now selling puts at 7475 and 7470, which makes them support again, and new buyers showed up at 7430 to 7440, exactly where Wednesday's drop stopped. The biggest position on Thursday's board is the seller between 7580 and 7610, put there to stop any big bounce. Levels are changing their character every day now.

Today can move fast in both directions, so trade smaller than usual. On the downside, support comes at 7475, then 7450, then 7430 to 7440. The drops between those levels can be quick because there is nothing in between. On the upside there is not much in the way between 7515 and 7550. If price gets going up there it can run to 7560 quickly. That is where the real resistance starts.

Jobless Claims at 8:30 AM sets the tone. It is the first job market data since the weak jobs report last week. A high number says the job market is cracking. A low number says it is holding up. Either way it hits a market already nervous about oil. On a calm day price tends to drift back to 7500, the busiest level on the board. Whether today gets to be calm depends on the war news.

Just in: early morning! Across the board, Goldman Sachs made a big statement! U.S. stocks, gold and silver rise collectively.

Early this morning, at the beginning of the Asia-Pacific session, major U.S. stock index futures rose across the board, with Nasdaq 100 index futures soaring by more than 1%; the precious metals market also strengthened collectively. Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest report that it has captured the initial dip buying signal of "U.S.

momentum stocks" and that the momentum factor has the basis for a tactical rebound. At the same time, international oil prices continued to fall, which also eased market concerns about the risk of rising inflation. On the news, OPEC+ has agreed to increase production quotas by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

Wall Street institutions such as Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs have recently warned that the international crude oil market may be at risk of oversupply next year. In the morning of July 6, Beijing time, the three major U.S. stock index futures collectively rose.

As of 7:20, Nasdaq 100 futures rose 1.45%, S&P 500 futures rose 0.5%, and Dow futures rose 0.04%. The precious metals market also rose slightly, with spot silver rising 1.2% to US$63.11 per ounce and spot gold rising 0.37% to US$4,189.54 per ounce. U.S.

stocks were closed for holidays last Friday, and the share prices of Korean chip giants rose sharply across the board. As of the close, SK Hynix rose 10.88% and Samsung Electronics rose 8.22%. In the previous two trading days, U.S. chip stocks fell sharply, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index falling by more than 11%.

The Goldman Sachs Trading Desk pointed out that the current decline in "U.S. momentum stocks" has provided room for short-term tactical rebounds - historical experience shows that the momentum factor often records positive returns during the correction phase.

However, Goldman Sachs reminded that momentum factor positions are still highly crowded, and if the deleveraging trend continues, the maximum potential retracement may still be twice the current decline.

Overall, the market is in the early stages of clearing momentum strategy positions, and the key to subsequent interpretation lies in the game between the liquidity environment and the rhythm of emotional repair.

Data from Goldman Sachs shows that the momentum factor has fallen by 24% since its peak, the largest retracement since the first quarter of 2023, which is significantly higher than the historical average retracement of about 12%. Looking at the adjustment cycle, the historical average lasts for about 24 days, while the current one lasts only 10 days.

Goldman Sachs pointed out that since 2026, high beta momentum strategies have experienced multiple losses of more than 10% within two days, and have since recovered to varying degrees. The current market trend is highly consistent with the previous model.

The bank believes that this round of amplified decline is mainly affected by structural factors such as low liquidity and weak summer trading, and is not a fundamental change in the fundamental trend.

Judging from historical experience, the winning rate of the bargain-hunting strategy has significantly improved after the Federal Reserve’s policy turns to easing (such as during the epidemic).

Goldman Sachs specifically mentioned that the market's doubts about the return rate on its capital expenditures caused by Meta's transformation to cloud business are similar to the path of impact on market sentiment caused by similar concerns in the past.

The bank's trading department currently judges that the core narrative of AI has not yet undergone structural changes that are sufficient to drive a deeper correction. International oil prices continue to fall International oil prices continued to fall.

As of 7:20 Beijing time, WTI crude oil futures fell 0.52% to US$68.33/barrel; Brent crude oil futures fell 0.61% to US$71.68/barrel. On the news, according to Bloomberg, OPEC+ has agreed to increase production quotas by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

This quota increase of 188,000 barrels per day will bring OPEC+’s cumulative new quotas since the outbreak of the war to 940,000 barrels per day, equivalent to nearly 1% of global demand. The above-mentioned production increase plan is jointly promoted by seven major member countries, led by Saudi Arabia and Russia.

According to previous reports by Bloomberg, OPEC+ has formulated a roadmap and plans to complete the full cancellation of the two rounds of production cuts in 2023 by September through continued quota increases.

The interim peace agreement between the United States and Iran has cleared key obstacles for the Persian Gulf oil-producing countries to resume exports.

According to tanker tracking data, oil exports from Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates have basically returned to pre-war levels, and the freight channels between the two countries through the Strait of Hormuz have been successfully opened. However, data compiled by Bloomberg show that the actual output of the two countries is still far below normal.

The current recovery in exports mainly relies on the digestion of early inventory, rather than the simultaneous recovery of production capacity. It will still take time to fully release output. At the same time, there are also variables in the situation in the Strait of Hormuz.

According to CCTV News, data released by the US-led Joint Maritime Information Center on the 5th showed that in the 72 hours from July 2 to 4, a total of 70 merchant ships were escorted by the US through the Strait of Hormuz. The scale of navigation was far lower than the average daily average of 138 ships before the conflict.

This data was released by the British Maritime Trade Action Office on the same day. The announcement shows that the daily traffic volume of U.S.-coordinated escort ships continues to decline: 33 ships on July 2, 29 ships on July 3, and only 18 ships on July 4.

There are currently two navigation channels in the Strait of Hormuz, namely the southern channel near Oman and the northern channel controlled by Iran. The traffic volume of the southern channel escorted by the United States has not continued to increase, and the actual total navigation volume of the northern channel is difficult to accurately calculate.

The announcement rated the threat level in the entire Strait of Hormuz as "high." Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps continues to carry out very high-frequency radio broadcasts, drone close-in reconnaissance and tracking surveillance on transiting merchant ships, and implements regular control of the Strait waterways; ships that continue to turn on the automatic ship identification system signal may receive warnings requiring diversion to Iranian-controlled waterways.

The announcement also stated that there is still a risk of mines in the Strait waters, and relevant mine clearance survey operations are being carried out across the entire area; interference to global navigation satellite systems in the entire area is normal and will continue to pose hidden dangers to the safety of ship navigation. 科创50成为主要宽基指数中唯一收红的指数，报2016.23点，涨0.73%。北证50表现最弱，跌2.59%。大盘股表现优于中小盘，市场风格偏向蓝筹。

沪深两市全天成交额约2.58万亿元，较前一交易日缩量约159亿元，已连续5个交易日缩量，创下4月8日以来的最低成交水平。全市场仅1593只个股上涨，3790只下跌，超3700只个股飘绿；56只涨停、44只跌停。市场情绪整体偏弱。

二、分时走势回顾

早盘：四大股指集体高开。开盘后指数一度快速下探，创业板指、深成指均一度跌超2%，但随后展开强势反弹。科创50率先发力，半日大涨3.13%，报2064.20点。截至午间收盘，沪指涨0.52%重返4000点，深成指涨0.08%，创业板指涨0.95%。半日成交额1.72万亿元，较上日放量736亿元。算力租赁、半导体芯片、油气等方向领涨。

午后：指数冲高后持续回落，三大股指由涨转跌。机器人、锂电池、有色金属等板块加速下挫，拖累主要股指转跌。最终三大指数集体收跌，科创50涨幅也由超3%收窄至0.73%，呈现典型的冲高回落走势。

🛢️ Trump Threatens Iran, Russia Halts Diesel Exports: Oil Market's Second Fire

Daily · 2026-07-09

The real story in oil today is not simply that crude is rising again.

Brent is back near the $80 area, but it has not turned into a $100 panic. That can make the energy shock look smaller than it is.

The market is not only repricing crude. It is repricing diesel, shipping lanes, refining capacity and refined-product exports.

━━━
▌1. Two battlefields are squeezing refined fuels

The first line is the Middle East.

At the NATO summit in Ankara, Donald Trump said the ceasefire with Iran was probably over. After Iran attacked commercial vessels, the U.S. also launched a new round of strikes around Iran and the Strait of Hormuz.

The market is not reacting only to rhetoric. It is asking whether Hormuz again becomes a route that can still be crossed, but at a much higher cost and risk.

The second line is Russia and Ukraine.

Russia, facing domestic fuel shortages, announced a halt to diesel exports until the end of July. The background is Ukraine's continued strikes on Russian refineries, including Saratov and Tatarstan.

This is not an abstract crude-supply issue. It directly affects diesel and refined-product availability.

〔Chart: Global oil chokepoints and Hormuz share〕
〔Chart: Hormuz tanker crossings〕

━━━
▌2. Crude has not broken loose because Saudi pricing and Asian demand cap the upside

If geopolitics were the only force, crude would probably be easier to push into a panic.

But today's tape also has a counterweight.

Saudi Arabia sharply cut August official selling prices for Asian buyers. Arab Light was set at a $1.50 per barrel discount to the Oman/Dubai average, down $11 from July.

That means the market is not only telling a tight-supply story.

Weak Asian demand, stronger competition among Gulf barrels, and higher freight and insurance costs are forcing producers to compete on price.

War and shipping risk pull prices up. Weak demand and discounted crude pull against that move. Crude is in a tug of war, but refined products are already tighter.

━━━
▌3. Diesel looks more like the real pressure gauge than Brent

The latest EIA data shows the split clearly.

U.S. commercial crude inventories rose by 3 million barrels, but distillate inventories fell by 5 million barrels to 103.6 million barrels, around 12% below the five-year average.

Crude barrels are not scarce enough to create a full panic, but diesel, heating oil and parts of the jet-fuel chain are closer to the shortage.

That is why crack spreads matter.

When diesel crack spreads rise quickly, the market is paying more to turn crude into usable fuel.

The pressure is not necessarily in the oil field. It is in refineries, inventories, ships, ports and final delivery.

〔Chart: Diesel crack spread〕

━━━
▌4. The U.S. is becoming the global refined-product buffer

U.S. petroleum product exports jumped to 8.73 million barrels per day in a week, near a two-year high.

The U.S. is not only a crude producer. Its refining system is helping plug global refined-product gaps.

When Russian diesel exports are restricted and Middle East shipping risk rises, the places that can turn crude into diesel, gasoline and jet fuel and then ship them out become more valuable.

But that buffer is not free.

If U.S. product exports stay high while distillate inventories fail to rebuild, the pressure will show up in diesel futures, crack spreads, freight and end-user fuel prices.

〔Chart: U.S. petroleum product exports〕

━━━
▌5. The deeper repricing is about energy-system flexibility

BP, Shell and TotalEnergies are not just oil producers. They own refineries, storage, terminals, ships, long-term contracts and trading desks.

When price gaps open between regions, products and delivery dates, those assets become options.

Consumers and transport operators absorb higher fuel costs. But companies with refining flexibility, inventories, ships and trading capacity can turn disorder into profit.

The market is not punishing everyone equally. It is redistributing value toward those who can move fuel to where it is most needed.

━━━
▌Four signals to watch next

1. Whether Trump's military pressure on Iran pushes Hormuz tanker flows lower again.

2. Whether Russia extends the diesel export halt beyond the end of July.

3. Whether Ukraine keeps expanding refinery strikes, turning Russia from an energy exporter into a source of refined-product stress.

4. Whether Saudi discounts actually attract Asian refiners, or instead confirm weaker Asian demand.

Today's judgment is simple: crude below $100 does not mean the oil market is fine.

The first fire is in diesel, crack spreads, transit rights and the ability to refine and deliver usable fuel.

Crude is the headline. Diesel is today's pressure gauge.

Sources: AP, Guardian, WSJ/EIA, WSJ, Economic Times/Reuters, EIA and public market data. Images from @jackprandelli, @Hedgeye, @Rory_Johnston and @zerohedge.

5/ Energy Supply Shock Volatility

Geopolitical risks intersecting with transition demand and traditional supply constraints are generating persistent swings in oil, gas and coal prices that feed directly into inflation and sector rotation. These shocks amplify earnings dispersion between energy producers and downstream consumers.

EIA REPORT: FUNDAMENTALS REMAIN STABLE, BUT GEOPOLITICAL RISK IS DRIVING THE OIL MARKET

The latest EIA Weekly Petroleum Status Report paints a clear picture: oil market fundamentals remain relatively balanced, while geopolitical risk in the Middle East has become the dominant force behind price action.

Here are the key takeaways:

1. U.S. crude inventories increased

Commercial crude oil inventories (excluding the SPR) rose by 3.0 million barrels, from 408.4 million to 411.4 million barrels.

Under normal market conditions, this would be considered a bearish signal, indicating that physical crude supply remains sufficient.

⸻

2. U.S. crude production remains near record highs

Domestic production reached approximately 13.86 million barrels per day, continuing to hover near historical highs.

The EIA also noted that this week’s figure includes a benchmark adjustment, meaning the increase reflects statistical revisions rather than a sudden surge in actual production.

Overall, U.S. supply remains robust.

⸻

3. Refineries are running at very high utilization

U.S. refinery utilization climbed to 95.8%.

This suggests refiners continue to operate at an exceptionally strong pace, supported by steady fuel demand and healthy refining margins.

⸻

4. Gasoline and distillate inventories declined

During the week:

Gasoline inventories fell by 1.9 million barrels
Distillate (diesel) inventories dropped by 5.0 million barrels

Distillates are often viewed as a key indicator of industrial activity and freight demand, making this a supportive signal for petroleum consumption.

⸻

5. Net imports tightened

U.S. crude imports declined by roughly 654,000 barrels per day, while exports remained elevated.

This helps offset inventory builds and could limit supply pressure in the coming weeks.

⸻

Fundamentals alone would not justify a major rally

If this had been an ordinary week, the report would likely have placed mild downward pressure on oil prices.

But this is not an ordinary market.

Traders are largely looking past inventory data because geopolitical developments have become the primary driver.

Among the major factors currently influencing prices are:

The United States revoking waivers related to Iranian oil exports.
Rising military tensions between the U.S. and Iran.
Confirmed military operations by CENTCOM.
Attacks involving commercial shipping near the Strait of Hormuz.
Continued uncertainty surrounding Kharg Island, the hub for roughly 90% of Iran’s crude exports.

In today’s environment, geopolitical risk premium outweighs weekly inventory data.

⸻

What is the market pricing now?

The market is no longer focused solely on current supply.

Instead, it is pricing the probability of future disruptions, including:

A disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
A sharp reduction in Iranian oil exports.
Expanded U.S. military operations.
Iranian retaliation against regional assets.
Supply disruptions affecting Gulf producers.

As long as the perceived probability of these events increases, oil prices can continue rising—even if inventories remain comfortable.

⸻

What to watch next

The next major drivers for crude prices are likely to be:

• Military developments involving the U.S. and Iran.

• Any activity around Kharg Island.

• Commercial shipping flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

• OPEC+ policy responses.

• Additional restrictions on Iranian oil exports.

⸻

Bottom line

The latest EIA report suggests that the physical oil market is not experiencing a severe supply shortage. U.S. crude production remains strong, commercial inventories increased, and refinery operations continue at a high level.

However, fundamentals are currently taking a back seat to geopolitics. As long as tensions in the Middle East continue to escalate, oil prices are likely to react far more to military developments than to weekly inventory reports.

For your education- The “petrodollar deal” between the United States and Saudi Arabia, established in 1974, which Saudi Arabia agreed to price and sell its oil exports predominantly in U.S. dollars. 
In return, the U.S. provided military protection, arms sales, and security guarantees to the Kingdom. This built on earlier oil-for-security ties dating back to 1945 (the Roosevelt-Ibn Saud meeting) but was formalized in the context of the 1973 oil crisis and the end of the Bretton Woods gold standard. 
After the 1971 Nixon Shock (ending dollar-gold convertibility) and the 1973 OPEC oil embargo (tied to the Yom Kippur War), the U.S. faced pressure on the dollar. Through diplomacy involving Henry Kissinger and others, the U.S. and Saudi Arabia reached agreements where:
•  Saudi oil would be priced and sold in USD (encouraging other OPEC nations to follow).
•  Saudi “petrodollars” (dollar revenues from oil) would be recycled into U.S. Treasuries and U.S. assets/military purchases.
•  The U.S. offered military aid, equipment, and protection for Saudi oil fields & military assets. This created the broader petrodollar system: global oil trade denominated in USD, driving worldwide demand for dollars. Oil exporters earn dollars, hold or invest them in dollar assets, and importers need dollars to buy oil. This drives up demand for USD worldwide.
